content_en:
The definition and criteria of the Sahih (sound/authentic) hadith constitute the most foundational chapter of the Muqaddimah, establishing the gold standard against which all other hadith types are measured. Ibn al-Salah provides what has become the canonical definition in hadith science: 'A sound hadith is one whose isnad (chain of transmission) is connected, narrated by just and precise narrators throughout, without shadhdhudhh (contradicting a stronger narration) and without illah (a hidden defect).'

This definition contains five conditions, each of which requires elaboration:

First, ittissal al-isnad (continuity of the chain): every narrator in the chain must have actually received the narration from the narrator before them — either through direct hearing (sima'), reading in the teacher's presence (qira'ah), writing, or another recognized mode of transmission. A 'mursal' narration (in which a Successor narrates from the Prophet ﷺ without mentioning an intermediary Companion) is not sahih according to the strict definition, though the majority of jurists treat certain types of mursal narrations as legally actionable.

Second, 'adalah (moral uprightness of narrators): every narrator in the chain must be a Muslim of known uprightness — meaning they are not known for major sins, minor sins committed openly, or conduct that violates the norms of propriety (muru'ah). A person whose moral character is unknown (majhul al-hal) or known to be problematic cannot narrate a sahih hadith.

Third, dabt (precision/accuracy of narrators): every narrator must have both memorized the narration accurately (dabt al-sadr) or preserved it in writing carefully (dabt al-kitab), and must have transmitted it without significant error or confusion. This condition is what distinguishes the work of true hadith masters (huffaz) from ordinary narrators.

Fourth, 'adam al-shadhdhudhh (absence of anomaly): the narration must not contradict a more reliable narration on the same subject. A narration reported by a single reliable narrator that contradicts what multiple narrators of equal or greater reliability have transmitted is called 'shadhdhah' and is classified separately.

Fifth, 'adam al-illah al-qadihah (absence of disqualifying hidden defect): sometimes a hadith appears sound by external examination but has a hidden defect (illah) discoverable only by the most expert hadith masters — such as a conflation of two narrations, a confusion between narrators of similar names, or a narration that appears muttasil (connected) but is in fact mursal. Detecting these defects requires the encyclopedic knowledge of the greatest hadith specialists.

The pre-eminent examples of sahih hadith collections are those of al-Bukhari and Muslim — al-Jami' al-Sahih of each. Imam al-Bukhari (194–256 AH) selected approximately 7,563 hadiths (with repetitions) from some 600,000 narrations he had examined, applying criteria so rigorous that the scholars have said about his collection: 'the most authentic book after the Book of Allah is the Sahih of al-Bukhari.' Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj (204–261 AH) selected approximately 12,000 hadiths (with repetitions) and organized his collection with exceptional systematization. Together, these two collections — Sahihayn — represent the highest level of hadith authentication in the Sunni tradition.
content_id:
Definisi dan kriteria hadis Shahih (sahih/autentik) merupakan bab paling fondasi dari Muqaddimah, yang menetapkan standar emas yang menjadi tolok ukur semua jenis hadis lainnya. Ibn ash-Shalah memberikan definisi yang telah menjadi definisi kanonik dalam ilmu hadis: "Hadis shahih adalah hadis yang sanadnya bersambung melalui periwayatan orang yang adil dan dhabith dari orang yang adil dan dhabith, sampai ke akhir sanad, tanpa syudzudz (kejanggalan) dan tanpa illah (cacat tersembunyi)."

Dari definisi ini, para ulama mengidentifikasi lima syarat hadis shahih:

Pertama: Ittishal as-sanad (ketersambungan sanad) — setiap perawi dalam rantai periwayatan harus benar-benar bertemu dan mengambil hadis langsung dari gurunya. Tidak boleh ada perawi yang majhul (tidak dikenal) atau perawi yang terputus hubungannya.

Kedua: 'Adalah ar-rawi (keadilan perawi) — setiap perawi harus seorang Muslim, baligh, berakal, tidak fasik, dan tidak melakukan hal-hal yang merusak kehormatan (muru'ah). Keadilan ini berkaitan dengan karakter moral perawinya.

Ketiga: Dhabt ar-rawi (kekuatan hafalan perawi) — perawi harus memiliki hafalan yang kuat dan akurat, baik hafalan dada (hafal di dalam hati) maupun hafalan kitab (terjaga dalam tulisan). Kekuatan hafalan ini dibuktikan melalui perbandingan dengan riwayat-riwayat lain.

Keempat: Khuluw min asy-syudzudz (bebas dari kejanggalan) — hadis tersebut tidak boleh bertentangan dengan riwayat perawi yang lebih terpercaya atau lebih banyak jumlahnya.

Kelima: Khuluw min al-illah (bebas dari cacat tersembunyi) — tidak ada cacat yang tidak tampak secara langsung tetapi dapat diidentifikasi melalui penelitian mendalam oleh para hafizh (ahli hafal hadis).

Hadis yang memenuhi kelima syarat ini disebut shahih li dzatihi. Jika sedikit lemah dalam hafalan tetapi memiliki jalur periwayatan lain yang menguatkannya, ia naik menjadi shahih li ghayrihi.
